data.title: Rezolve Ai reaffirms commitment to $1B Bitcoin treasury strategy
data.description:
• Rezolve Ai (NASDAQ:RZLV) on Thursday reaffirmed its commitment to executing its previously announced $1B Bitcoin-backed treasury strategy. 
  • Rezolve’s vision is to embed Bitcoin-backed liquidity directly into its AI-powered checkout and payment infrastructure, allowing consumers to spend crypto in real time. 
  • The Bitcoin Treasury remains an active and strategic priority for Rezolve Ai. The company expects to announce further progress in the coming months.
  • Rezolve Ai (NASDAQ:RZLV) shares were last up more than 12%.

data.title: Chagee returns to Vietnam after riding out a boycott
data.description:
Chinese milk tea chain Chagee (NASDAQ:CHA) has turned heads in the beverage sector with a significant return to Vietnam after a hiatus that lasted several months due to a controversy.

The brand originally attempted to enter the Vietnamese market in early 2025, selecting a flagship store location at the intersection of Dong Khoi and Nguyen Thiep in Ho Chi Minh City. However, Chagee (NASDAQ:CHA) faced widespread criticism and a consumer boycott due to the presence of a controversial "nine-dash line" map in its app, prompting the company to suspend its launch and remove branding from its prime location.

The nine-dash line is considered controversial because it represents China's expansive maritime claim over nearly 90% of the South China Sea, which is a region already claimed in part by several Southeast Asian countries, including Vietnam, the Philippines, Malaysia, Brunei, and Indonesia.

After four months of silence, Chagee officially opened its first Vietnamese store on July 16, signaling its return and a change in strategy. The reopening was supported by a campaign of promotional discounts intended to rebuild trust and win back customers. Chagee has also updated its digital platforms for the Vietnam market, now featuring the S-shaped national map of Vietnam, to address previous concerns over sovereignty.

Despite the company's efforts, public skepticism reportedly remains high. There has been a social media push in Vietnam urging support for local tea brands over Chagee (CHA). 

Looking further down the road, Chagee's (CHA) expansion plan includes establishing multiple new stores in Ho Chi Minh City, aiming to compete in the vibrant local tea market.

Shares of Chagee Holdings (CHA) trade just above $26, which is below the IPO pricing level of $28.

data.title: Elevance Health outlines $30 EPS target for 2025 amid ACA and Medicaid cost pressures
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Elevance Health (ELV) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Gail Koziara Boudreaux addressed the company's revised full year outlook, stating, "We know this adjustment is disappointing, and we're taking concrete actions to address it." She emphasized the company's focus on "execution, making the right decisions now to strengthen the business and position Elevance Health for long-term sustainable performance."
  • Boudreaux highlighted continued strength in the Medicare Advantage portfolio and disciplined cost management while noting challenges in the ACA and Medicaid markets. She stated, "Our Carillon platform continues to drive growth. Carelon Rx is gaining traction...and Carelon Services delivered strong results with CareBridge scaling rapidly across dual eligible and high-acuity Medicaid populations."
  • The CEO announced a revision to the full year 2025 adjusted EPS guidance to approximately $30, attributing the change to "elevated medical cost trends across ACA and slower-than-expected Medicaid rate alignment."
  • Boudreaux described ongoing investments in AI-enabled tools, value-based care expansion, and streamlined prior authorization processes as integral to the company's multiyear margin stabilization plan. She noted, "More than 1/3 of our benefit expense is now in downside risk arrangements."
  • CFO Mark Bradley Kaye stated, "Elevance Health reported second quarter GAAP diluted earnings per share of $7.72 and adjusted diluted earnings per share of $8.84." He explained the updated full year guidance reflects "both an industry-wide increase in morbidity impacting our ACA business and a slower deceleration in Medicaid cost trend."

Outlook

  • Management revised the full year 2025 adjusted EPS outlook to approximately $30. Boudreaux explained, "This reset reflects the same pressures that others in the sector have now confirmed, particularly elevated medical cost trends across ACA and slower-than-expected Medicaid rate alignment."
  • Kaye stated the benefit expense ratio forecast is now "approximately 90% for the full year as elevated trend levels persist in our ACA and Medicaid businesses."
  • Management is not basing assumptions on a near-term recovery, with Boudreaux stating, "It is not based on assumptions of a near-term recovery. We are choosing to act now, not later to ensure our outlook reflects prevailing conditions."

Financial Results

  • Kaye reported operating revenue of $49.4 billion for the quarter, an increase of 14% year-over-year, driven by higher premium yields and acquisitions in home health and specialty pharmacy.
  • The company ended the second quarter with 45.6 million medical members, down approximately 200,000 sequentially, attributed to Medicaid membership reductions and lower effectuation rates in ACA.
  • The consolidated benefit expense ratio was 88.9%, up 260 basis points year-over-year, primarily due to ACA and Medicaid businesses.
  • Carelon Rx grew operating revenue by over 20%, and Carelon Services delivered greater than 50% growth in revenue and operating gain, with profitability impacted by investments for growth.
  • The debt-to-capital ratio stood at 40.8%. Year-to-date, Elevance Health returned approximately $2 billion to investors. Operating cash flow for the quarter totaled $2.1 billion, with full year expectations now at approximately $6 billion.

Q&A

  • Andrew Mok, Barclays: Asked about ACA cost pressures and risk adjustment expectations. Kaye explained that the "sharper uptick on medical trend in our ACA book" is mostly due to higher risk pool acuity and morbidity, with utilization and aggressive provider coding also contributing.
  • Albert J. William Rice, UBS: Inquired about the impact split between ACA and Medicaid on the guidance revision and Medicaid margin recovery timing. Boudreaux stated the revision is "slightly more weighted towards ACA" and described Medicaid recovery as a "more gradual alignment to rates" due to increased morbidity from enrollment losses.
  • Stephen C. Baxter, Wells Fargo: Questioned Medicaid rate disconnects and forward trend. Boudreaux noted that states have been "very responsive" to data sharing and expects normalization "over a couple of rate cycles."
  • Lisa Christine Gill, JPMorgan: Asked about Medicare Advantage trends and bids for 2026. Boudreaux said MA trends were "in line with expectations," and Felicia Farr Norwood described the bid strategy as disciplined, focused on retention, sustainability, and margin stabilization.
  • Justin Lake, Wolfe Research: Asked about Medicaid margin levels and potential for above 12% long-range plan growth in 2026. Kaye indicated operating margin in ACA is expected to decline year-over-year in the high single-digit percent range, and while no formal guidance was given, Boudreaux emphasized ongoing disciplined pricing and cost management.
  • Erin Elizabeth Wilson Wright, Morgan Stanley: Asked about cost structure initiatives. Boudreaux outlined ongoing automation, AI deployment, and aggressive action against billing abuses as current priorities.
  • Multiple analysts pressed for clarity on risk pool shifts, coding practices, utilization trends, capital allocation, and the company’s approach to stabilizing market volatility.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative, with repeated questions focused on the sustainability of revised guidance, visibility into Medicaid and ACA headwinds, and the company’s ability to restore margin stability. Analysts expressed skepticism regarding the persistence of elevated cost trends and the risk of further downside.
  • Management maintained a neutral to slightly defensive tone, emphasizing transparency and decisive action. Boudreaux acknowledged disappointment in revising guidance, but expressed confidence in the company’s long-term strategy: "We remain confident in the strength of our enterprise, the impact of our investments and our ability to create long-term value."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analyst and management tone shifted from cautious optimism to a more defensive posture, with greater focus on headwinds and less discussion of upside drivers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was revised down from a previous range of $34.15 to $34.85 to approximately $30 adjusted EPS for 2025. Management’s language on outlook became more conservative, citing persistent cost pressures in ACA and Medicaid versus earlier expectations of improvement.
  • Strategic focus shifted from expansion and operational momentum in Q1 to margin stabilization and risk management in Q2.
  • Analysts in Q2 expressed increased concern about cost trends, risk pool deterioration, and the timeline for Medicaid margin recovery, in contrast to a more balanced inquiry set in Q1.
  • Key metric changes included a decline in medical membership and a higher benefit expense ratio, compared to sequential growth and a lower ratio the prior quarter.
  • Management’s confidence in Carelon and technology investments remained, but there was less emphasis on growth and more on controlling factors within their influence.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted "elevated medical cost trends across ACA and slower-than-expected Medicaid rate alignment" as ongoing challenges.
  • There are persistent risks related to provider coding practices, inappropriate use of the No Surprises Act dispute resolution process, and the impact of policy changes such as Medicaid work requirements and the expiration of enhanced marketplace subsidies.
  • Analyst concerns focused on the potential for further downside risk if cost trends do not stabilize, and whether rate updates will adequately reflect current acuity in Medicaid.
  • Management is pursuing legal action against billing abuses, expanding use of analytics to detect fraud, and intensifying cost management and pricing discipline to mitigate these risks.

Final Takeaway

Elevance Health management reiterated its commitment to transparency and execution amid a challenging operating environment. The company is prioritizing actions to stabilize trends in ACA and Medicaid, enhance pricing alignment, and invest in technology-driven care and payment integrity. While the reset in full year EPS guidance to $30 reflects persistent industry-wide cost headwinds, management stated these steps are intended to restore long-term margin stability and position the enterprise for sustainable growth as market dynamics evolve.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: AstraZeneca, Daiichi Sankyo get U.S. breakthrough therapy status for Enhertu
data.description:
• AstraZeneca (NASDAQ:AZN) and Daiichi Sankyo's drug Enhertu, in combination with pertuzumab, has received breakthrough therapy status in the U.S. for treating adults with advanced HER2 positive breast cancer that cannot be surgically removed.
  • Enhertu is an antibody drug that targets HER2,  developed by Daiichi Sankyo and AstraZeneca together.

data.title: Wall Steet moves higher after the latest retail sales report and further earnings data
data.description:
Wall Street’s market averages trade higher on Thursday, as investors digested fresh data on retail sales and jobless claims.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) has pushed up by 0.3% and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) has climbed by 0.2%. At the same time, the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) has advanced by 0.6%. 

Among the 11 S&P 500 sectors, seven are up in the green, with Info Tech and Industrials leading the way. At the other end of the spectrum, Health Care has been the area that has suffered the most so far. 

U.S. Treasury yields appear to be shifting slightly lower with the shorter-end U.S. 2 Year Treasury yield (US2Y) near even at 3.90% and the longer-end U.S. 10 Year Treasury yield (US10Y) has fallen 2 basis points to 4.43%. 

Looking out at the economic calendar and June retail sales came in at +0.6% M/M to $720.1B vs. +0.1% consensus and -0.9% prior (unrevised), while core retail sales, excluding motor vehicles and parts, came in at +0.5% M/M vs. +0.3% consensus and -0.6% prior (revised from -0.3%). 

“Strong June retail sales show consumers may have been spooked by tariffs, but they haven't fully gone into hiding. The fact that June is a slower month and that goods outlays comprise a fraction of overall outlays means that we are not completely rethinking our forecast for a soft second half for consumer spending,” Wells Fargo’s Tim Quinlan said. 

Furthermore, initial jobless claims unexpectedly fell in the past week. Business inventories also held steady again in May at 0.0% M/M to $2,656.7B. Additionally, homebuilder sentiment ticked up one point in July, as was expected. 

“The VIX eased slightly overnight. Despite the back-and-forth headlines concerning the possible sacking of Fed Chair Powell and President Trump’s trade wars, market participants ultimately didn’t panic, leaving implied volatility apparently well anchored. The muted reaction to yesterday’s events suggests that traders remain focused on earnings and macro data rather than political theatre, at least for now,” David Morrison of Trade Nation said. 

Earnings remain in focus, with Travelers Corp, Citizens Financial and US Bancorp reporting. Netflix will headline after hours, offering a tech-sector litmus test amid rising subscriber growth expectations, Morrison added. 

Streaming giant Netflix (NFLX) will release its quarterly results after the closing bell.

data.title: DiaMedica gains on positive mid-stage trial data for lead drug in preeclampsia
data.description:
• DiaMedica Therapeutics (NASDAQ:DMAC) traded higher on Thursday after the company announced that a Phase 2 trial for its lead asset DM199 reached its key goals against preeclampsia, a pregnancy disorder characterized by high blood pressure.
  • Citing interim results, the Minneapolis, Minnesota-based biotech said that the trial’s Part 1a dose escalation phase achieved pre-specified safety and efficacy endpoints.
  • Commenting on blood pressure reduction, DiaMedica (NASDAQ:DMAC) noted that patients in pooled cohorts 6–9 who were subject to the potentially therapeutic dose range experienced durable mean blood pressure reductions with a statistically significant effect.
  • The open-label, single-arm trial conducted at the Tygerberg Hospital, Cape Town, South Africa, also indicated no serious treatment-emergent adverse events and no findings to suggest placental transfer of DM199.
  • The investigator-sponsored trial will move towards enrollment in the dose expansion cohort (Part 1b).

data.title: Marsh & McLennan Q2 earnings beat reflects acquisition tailwinds, business momentum
data.description:
Marsh & McLennan Companies (NYSE:MMC) posted Thursday second-quarter earnings that rose past the average analyst estimate as revenue momentum continued across its business units, along with acquisition tailwinds.

"We had another solid quarter with 12% revenue growth reflecting continued momentum across our business and the contribution from acquisitions," said President and CEO John Doyle.

Q2 2025 adjusted EPS of $2.72, exceeding the $2.66 consensus, retreated from $3.03 in the earlier quarter, but rose from $2.27 a year ago.

Revenue of $6.97B in Q2, vs. $6.95B expected, slid from $7.06B in Q1, but rose from $6.22B in Q2 2024.

Operating expenses were $5.15B in Q2, up from $5.06B in the earlier quarter and $4.58B a year before.

Adjusted operating income for the company's Risk & Insurance Services unit rose to $1.64B in Q2 from $1.81B in Q1 and +16% Y/Y.

During the quarter, MMC bought back 1.4M shares for $300M. Through six months ended June 30, 2025, the company has repurchased 2.7M shares of stock for $600M.

data.title: Potential Powell heir Warsh calls Fed independence 'essential' and touts regime change
data.description:
Former Federal Reserve Governor Kevin Warsh, who could replace current chair Jerome Powell, believes the Federal Reserve has mishandled interest rates and faces a credibility deficit due to changing policy goalposts.

In an interview with CNBC, Warsh emphasized that the Fed’s current problems stem primarily from its own mistakes rather than external criticism.

Warsh's comments come at a time when the independence of the central bank has become a key concern for market participants. Warsh emerged as a potential top contender to replace Powell back in April. Powell's term as Fed chief is set to expire in May 2026.

U.S. President Donald Trump has repeatedly criticized Powell for not lowering interest rates. Media reports on Wednesday said Trump was likely to fire Powell soon, which led to a brief slide in U.S. stocks. Trump later said it was "highly unlikely" he would remove Powell.  

“I’ve strongly believed for 20 years…that the independent operations in the conduct of monetary policy is essential,” Warsh told CNBC.

However, he argued that this independence does not extend to everything the Fed does. He argued that the current Fed overstepped its boundaries by venturing into areas like climate change and diversity, equity, and inclusion — subjects he believes have “no comparative advantage.”

According to Warsh, the Fed has been inconsistent in its approach, encouraging Congress to “spend, spend, spend” during one administration, while claiming spending constraints are not their concern during another.

He called for a “regime change” similar to Paul Volcker’s approach (who served from 1979 to 1987) that restored the institution’s credibility.

According to Warsh, the current Fed leadership hasn't “had interest rates about right for a long time, and they don’t really look, as they change goalposts, like they have control.”

Despite his criticisms, Warsh expressed optimism about the Fed’s potential for revival. “I’m a Fed guy,” he said, “I think it can be fixed. It was a great institution, and to use a cliché, I think it could be great again.”

Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU)

data.title: Resilience in Europe, outlook for cocoa futures earns upgrade for Mondelez
data.description:
Mondelez (NASDAQ:MDLZ) is showing resilience in Europe, with minimal demand erosion despite price increases. Supported by solid execution and a more favorable cocoa price outlook, Jefferies upgraded the stock to Buy from Hold and raised its price target to $78.

“Europe remains a key growth engine [for Mondelez] and we forecast [double-digit %] organic growth in Q2/rest of 2025, with upside possible should limited volume declines enable more cost recovery than expected,” Jefferies analyst Scott Marks wrote in his research note to clients.

In the U.S., however, demand was still subdued due to ongoing consumer strain with channel data for the twelve weeks ending June 14 showing sales down low-single-digits, slightly worse than the prior period. Volume share has followed suit, down 32 basis points year over year versus a 5 basis point improvement during the first quarter, as share loss in cookies has accelerated.

But while North American volumes could remain soft for the rest of the year, Marks believes Mondelez (NASDAQ:MDLZ) has been doing more than peers in terms of revenue growth management, which includes innovation, channel mix, pricing/promotion, and price-pack-architecture.

Looking at a key ingredient for Mondelez, cocoa futures' outlook will improve in 2026 which suggests Mondelez’s cost environment is also improving. Coupled with advantaged end market category positioning, pricing power, brand support, and revenue growth management capabilities, Jefferies believes the company has upside earnings potential over the next three years versus peers.

The outlook leads the analyst to raise his EPS estimates for Mondelez (NASDAQ:MDLZ) for Q3 to $0.81, to $0.79 for Q4 and to $3.03 for FY25, from $0.79, $0.78, and $3.00, respectively.

Mondelez (MDLZ) reports Q2 results after the market close on July 29, expected to show an adjusted profit of $0.68 per share on $8.81B in sales. 

Shares are up 2.3%.

data.title: Market Voices: Lucid-Uber robotaxis, Warsh on Fed "regime change"
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's daily roundup of statements, announcements and remarks that could impact markets or individual stocks. 

  • Uber (NYSE:UBER) said it plans to invest millions in EV maker Lucid (NASDAQ:LCID) as part of a new partnership to expand its robotaxi offerings.

Under the deal, Lucid will supply Uber with more than 20,000 EVs over a six-year period that utilize autonomous driving technology developed by Google-backed startup Nuro. Uber and Lucid plan to launch the robotaxi service next year.  

According to CNBC, the investment will be worth around $300 million. 

“We’re thrilled to partner with Nuro and Lucid on this new robotaxi program, purpose-built just for the Uber platform, to safely bring the magic of autonomous driving to more people across the world,” said Uber (NYSE:UBER) CEO Dara Khosrowshahi in a statement.

"This investment from Uber further validates Lucid's fully redundant zonal architecture and highly capable platform as ideal for autonomous vehicles, and our industry-leading range and spacious, well-appointed interiors, as ideal for ridesharing," said Lucid (NASDAQ:LCID) interim CEO Marc Winterhoff in the same statement. "This is the start of our path to extend our innovation and technology leadership into this multi-trillion-dollar market."

Uber (UBER) also has a robotaxi partnership with Google's (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) Waymo. 

  • Former Federal Reserve Governor Kevin Warsh, who is believed to be a leading candidate to replace Jerome Powell as Fed chair, is calling for "regime change" at the central bank.

“We need regime change in the conduct of policy,” Warsh said during an interview with CNBC on Thursday. “The credibility deficit lies with the incumbents that are at the Fed, in my view.”

Warsh also indicated that he would be at odds with several of the Federal Reserve governors if he were to become chair.

“Their hesitancy to cut rates, I think, is actually quite a quite a mark against them,” Warsh said during the interview. “The specter of the miss they made on inflation, it has stuck with them. So one of the reasons why the President, I think, is right to be pushing the Fed publicly is we need regime change in the conduct of policy.”
